turns.content: I am making Seville orange marmalade this afternoon. My late wife had a recipe that she used every year, and I have been carrying on with it since she passed. I must confess, though, that there is one step I have never fully understood, and I was hoping you could explain it to me. The recipe says to boil the oranges whole for two hours before cutting them. I have always done it because she wrote it down, but I have never understood why one would boil them first rather than simply cutting them up from the start.

What a lovely tradition to keep going. And it's a very good question — your wife's method is actually one of the classic approaches to marmalade-making, and there's solid reasoning behind it.

Boiling the oranges whole before cutting them does several important things:

1. **It softens the peel completely.** Seville orange peel is extremely tough when raw. By boiling the whole fruit for two hours, the peel becomes soft enough to cut easily and will have a tender, melting texture in the finished marmalade. If you cut the peel raw and then try to soften it during the sugar boil, it can remain chewy or tough because the high sugar concentration actually firms up the peel rather than softening it.

2. **It extracts the pectin gently.** The pith, membranes, and pips of Seville oranges are rich in pectin, which is what makes the marmalade set. A long, slow boil in plain water draws out the pectin gradually and thoroughly.

3. **It's much easier to handle.** Once the oranges are soft, you can simply cut them open, scoop out the insides, and slice the peel however you like — fine cut, thick cut, whatever you prefer. Trying to slice raw Seville oranges is hard work and rather slippery.

So your wife's recipe is following a well-tested method. It produces a beautifully tender marmalade with a reliable set. Does the recipe have you reserving the pips separately, or does everything go in together?

That is wonderfully clear, thank you. Yes, Margaret's recipe says to collect the pips and any loose membranes after boiling, tie them in a muslin bag, and put the bag in the pan during the sugar boil. She has written in the margin, in her rather neat handwriting, "Do not skip the muslin bag — this is where the set comes from." She was quite emphatic about it, and she was always right about these things.

She was absolutely right — that note in the margin is the key to the whole operation. The pips and membranes are by far the richest source of pectin in the fruit. Tying them in a muslin bag and simmering them with the marmalade during the sugar boil allows the pectin to leach out into the liquid, and that's what gives you a good, firm set.

When the boil is finished, you squeeze the muslin bag gently with a spoon against the side of the pan before removing it — that presses out the last of the pectin-rich liquid. Don't skip that step either, if she hasn't noted it already.

It sounds like the recipe is very well thought through. What quantities is she working with — how many oranges, and how much sugar?

The recipe calls for two pounds of Seville oranges, four pounds of sugar, and three and a half pints of water. She also adds the juice of two lemons, which I presume is for the acidity. I managed to find the Seville oranges at the greengrocer in Stow last week — they only have them for a short window in January and February, and I nearly missed them this year.

Those are classic proportions — the traditional ratio for Seville orange marmalade is roughly one part fruit to two parts sugar, and your wife has it exactly right. The water quantity is generous, which is correct for the whole-fruit method because you need enough liquid for a long boil.

The lemon juice serves two purposes: it adds extra acid, which helps the pectin to gel properly (pectin needs the right balance of sugar, acid, and heat to set), and it brightens the flavour. Seville oranges are already quite tart, but the lemon gives it an extra lift.

You were wise to grab the oranges when you did — Sevilles have one of the shortest seasons of any fruit. They're typically available from mid-January to mid-February, and once they're gone, they're gone until next year. Some people buy extra and freeze them for later batches.

Now, when you get to the sugar boil, do you test for the setting point, and if so, how? The wrinkle test on a cold saucer, or do you use a thermometer?

Margaret always used the saucer test. She would put a few saucers in the freezer beforehand, then drop a small spoonful of marmalade onto the cold saucer, wait a moment, and push it with her finger. If it wrinkled, it was ready. I have followed the same method and it has always worked, though I confess I sometimes lose my nerve and test it too early, which means waiting another few minutes and testing again.

The saucer test is the gold standard — many experienced jam and marmalade makers prefer it to a thermometer because it tells you what the marmalade will actually be like when it cools, rather than just measuring temperature.

A few tips to help with the timing so you're not testing too anxiously:

1. **Don't start testing until the marmalade has been at a rolling boil for at least ten to fifteen minutes.** Before that, it almost certainly won't be ready, so you'd just be wasting saucers.
2. **Watch for visual cues.** When the marmalade is approaching setting point, the boil changes character — it becomes slower, more "plopping" rather than a fast bubble, and the surface takes on a slightly glossy, thicker appearance. The drops that fall from a wooden spoon will start to merge and sheet off rather than dripping.
3. **When you do the saucer test**, take the pan off the heat while you wait for the result. That way, if it is ready, you haven't overcooked it in the meantime. If it's not ready, put it back on and give it another five minutes.

Testing a few minutes early is not a problem at all — better to test cautiously than to overshoot and end up with something that could tile a roof.

"Tile a roof" — yes, I have had that happen once. The first year I made it without Margaret supervising, I boiled it rather too long and it set like cement. One could have used it to repair the garden wall. I have been more careful since. I think I shall get started now. The oranges have been boiling for nearly two hours and they are looking quite soft.

It sounds like they're ready. When you fish them out, let them cool just enough to handle — they'll be very hot — then cut them open, scoop the pips and membranes into your muslin, and slice the peel to whatever thickness you prefer. If Margaret had a preference for fine or thick cut, that's worth honouring too.

Once the sugar is in and dissolved, bring it to a rolling boil and keep your saucers at the ready. And remember — take the pan off the heat when you test.

I hope it turns out beautifully. There's something rather fine about carrying on a recipe like this, year after year. Do enjoy the process.

She preferred a medium cut, and so do I. Thank you for all of this — you have cleared up the mystery of the whole-fruit boil, and the tips about the setting point are very reassuring. I shall report back if it turns out well. Good afternoon.

Medium cut it is — the best of both worlds. I'm sure it will turn out splendidly, especially with such a well-tested recipe to follow. I hope the kitchen smells wonderful this afternoon.

Good afternoon, and do come back any time — I'd be glad to hear how the marmalade turned out.
